content:
Member-only story

Web Development

Responsive Design

Coding

React

Front End Development

4 min read
2 hours ago

--

I used to think I understood responsive design. Three years of designing for mobile, tablet, and desktop breakpoints in Figma will do that to you. Then I started actually building, and realized designing for different screens and making a website behave across them are two completely different skills. The visual instincts carried over easily, typography, hierarchy, spacing all felt familiar. What didn’t carry over was watching a layout you designed carefully fall apart the moment a browser window resizes in ways Figma never showed you.
That gap is exactly what pushed me to slow down and actually understand responsive design instead of eyeballing it. Not just “does it look fine on my laptop,” but the actual tools and habits that make a layout hold up everywhere, from a small phone screen to an ultrawide monitor. Along the way I picked up a handful of practices that made the biggest difference, some obvious in hindsight, some I wish someone had told me earlier.
Here’s what I’ve learned so far.

1. Mobile-first
When building a website, always go from the smaller device to the bigger device. Developing for web is exciting, but the real problem shows up when you try to scale it down. The spacing, font size, gaps, and margins all become too much to handle, and many…
